video_title: AI Cracks: Where Do You Hide?
gist: Two traders (Avi, host; Jonah, guest) argue the AI/hyperscaler trade is in its late innings and that the next leg is shorting hyperscaler stock/credit while owning what they buy (memory, optoelectronics, energy) and, increasingly, the real-economy beneficiaries of AI like biotech. They are aggressively bearish on crypto near-term, expecting a Saylor/STRC blow-up that sets up a generational Bitcoin buy, and frame socialism/New York politics as a capital-flows risk to set-and-forget equity positions.

themes.theme: AI trade late innings & where to rotate

insights.claim: Jonah argues the market leadership has rotated from hyperscaler stocks themselves to what hyperscalers spend on, so investors should own the downstream beneficiaries (data-center inputs) rather than Google/Meta/Amazon shares directly.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Suggests shifting AI exposure from mega-cap names to second-derivative plays (memory, optoelectronics, energy) that are now driving the index higher.
insights.quote: the things that are holding up the market are where those hyperscalers are putting their money now, not necessarily the Google, Meta uh you know, Amazon price action themselves

insights.claim: Jonah frames the AI trade in three legs: hyperscaler stocks (done), what hyperscalers spend on (current leg, e.g. optoelectronics/energy/fiber), and the real-economy industries AI will fundamentally change (the next place to hide).
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Gives a sequencing map for rotating capital as each leg saturates; the 'third leg' (AI's real-economy effects) is the under-owned opportunity.
insights.quote: to me, that represents the second leg. And then the third leg is when you have, you know, like fiber optic cables go crazy

insights.claim: Hyperscaler free cash flow is collapsing toward zero after a roughly two-decade uptrend because of AI capex, including Google's ~$80B equity raise and an industry-wide $600-700B build-out.
insights.speaker: host & guest
insights.so_what: Spending intensity makes the mega-caps 'less sexy' and is a setup for sentiment-driven pullbacks even if the secular trade continues.
insights.quote: hyperscaler free cash flow after like a steady almost two decade uptrend is like going down to zero because what they're spending on AI build out

insights.claim: Avi compares today's white-hot memory/Nvidia names to the Nasdaq in 1999, arguing there is still substantial room to run but that violent pullbacks are inevitable, so uninvested traders should sit on the sidelines rather than chase.
insights.speaker: Avi (host)
insights.so_what: Buy dips only with high conviction; otherwise treat the rally as late-stage and avoid initiating fresh exposure into euphoria.
insights.quote: I I view this as like white hot upside volatility that these stocks are exhibiting the behavior of the Nasdaq in 1999

insights.claim: Jonah is short Accenture as an explicit AI-disruption trade, arguing consulting firms will see declining demand as AI substitutes for their work.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: A concrete single-name short expressing the 'who gets disrupted by AI' side of the thesis.
insights.quote: Great example of this is shorting Accenture. Right? Consulting firms are probably going to be less in demand.

themes.theme: Buy what hyperscalers buy, short what they sell

insights.claim: Jonah's capital-flows framework is to short hyperscaler stock and corporate credit (which they issue to fund purchases) while buying the data-center assets they consume.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Pairs a short on the funding side (equity/credit) with a long on the demand side (physical inputs) to isolate the AI build-out tailwind.
insights.quote: sell their stock, sell their sell their corporate debt, buy what they're buying, which is like data center assets

insights.claim: Jonah cautions that 'buy what hyperscalers buy' is not as simple as buying natural-gas utilities and optoelectronics, because what they actually purchase (e.g. high-bandwidth memory chips) cannot be directly owned and proxies like Micron stock have already rallied and are very volatile.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Warns against naive proxy trades; hyperscalers buy Micron's products, not its stock, so the proxy can diverge from the underlying demand.
insights.quote: Google, Microsoft, Meta, they're not buying Micron's stock. They're buying Micron's products. So, be careful there.

themes.theme: Bubble-saturation read on memory vs hyperscalers

insights.claim: Avi uses a retail-saturation tell: a 13-year-old holding single-name hyperscalers (Google, Uber, plus the rest) signals the hyperscaler trade may be over, while the same retail crowd has not yet bought memory names like SanDisk/Intel, implying the memory trade still has room.
insights.speaker: Avi (host)
insights.so_what: Sentiment dispersion argues for rotating out of saturated mega-caps into the still-under-owned memory trade.
insights.quote: What I would expect to see peak bubble is like every 13-year-old is buying SanDisk in their account, right? And he he hadn't he hadn't bought any SanDisk.

insights.claim: Both hosts argue the market is not at peak euphoria yet, citing forward P/E ratios on component makers like memory stocks and Intel/SanDisk and the absence of taxi-driver/retail crypto-style mania in those names.
insights.speaker: host & guest
insights.so_what: Implies the AI/memory rally has further upside before classic top signals appear; not yet time to fully de-risk equities.
insights.quote: I don't think we're at peak euphoria yet just based on price to price to forward earnings ratios, especially for the um the component makers like the memory stocks

insights.claim: Jonah argues volatility behavior is asset-class specific: equity vol rises on price declines (panic) except in extreme euphoria like 1999, crypto vol is a barbell (spikes on both euphoric rallies and 2022-style unwinds), and oil vol is stable in dollars-per-barrel rather than percentage terms.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Tells investors how to model risk and read signals differently by asset; mis-modeling oil vol in percent terms has historically ruined market makers.
insights.quote: volatility is quite stable in oil if you model it in dollars per barrel instead of percentage, because it turns out that a barrel of oil tends to move about as many dollars and cents per day whether it's trading 30 bucks or 100 bucks

insights.claim: Jonah argues heightened upside volatility only occurs during extreme greed, so when an asset shows white-hot upside vol it is a signal to lighten positions rather than add.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: A timing rule: upside vol spikes flag late-cycle greed and a window to trim exposure.
insights.quote: you're not going to get heightened upside volatility on mild greed. You're going to get heightened upside volatility on extreme greed

insights.claim: Avi argues technical analysis works best where an asset has heavy retail participation and weak fundamentals, which is why Bitcoin respected technicals for years until quants took over, and why momentum/breakout strategies have a high hit rate in high-vol crypto regimes.
insights.speaker: Avi (host)
insights.so_what: Defines when chart-based trading has edge (retail-driven, fundamental-light assets) versus equities where high vol means chop.
insights.quote: buying breakouts tends to be very good in crypto. That tends to be a very high hit rate strategy. If something trades above all-time highs, you buy it

insights.claim: Avi notes the Nasdaq has effectively gone sideways since roughly May 14-15 with elevated volatility, fitting the equity pattern where chop and high vol coincide when participants are uncertain.
insights.speaker: Avi (host)
insights.so_what: Sideways-but-volatile price action signals indecision rather than trend; favors patience over fresh directional bets.
insights.quote: basically Nasdaq's gone sideways since May 15th... we're kind of at those prices and volatility's been extremely high

insights.claim: Jonah declares Bitcoin and Ethereum 'cooked' near-term, arguing that as long as a higher-vol, more interesting trade exists (AI/memory stocks that can move 40% in days), capital will not flow into crypto.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Opportunity cost from AI volatility is the mechanism keeping crypto bid-less; stay short or on the sidelines until that rotates.
insights.quote: As long as there is a trade that has higher vol, that is more interesting than crypto, you just can't buy Bitcoin. And that's AI right now.

insights.claim: Jonah argues STRC (Strategy's 'Stretch' dividend product) is depegging because Saylor can only fund the dividend by selling STRC, MSTR, or Bitcoin, and each option is self-defeating, making an eventual dividend pause and product failure likely.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: The depeg prices the probability Saylor pauses the dividend; a forced unwind is a catalyst for both the product and Bitcoin lower.
insights.quote: STRC is depegging. And the reason that it's depegging is because basically, the way that it works is in order to pay the dividend, you sort of need to do well, really one of three things

insights.claim: Both hosts expect a Saylor/MSTR blow-up they call near-inevitable, after which Bitcoin's long-term value proposition and regulatory backdrop will be the best in history, setting up a generational buy near FTX-style lows.
insights.speaker: host & guest
insights.so_what: The thesis is to wait for the forced-seller capitulation, then buy the trough as 'the best trade of a lifetime' rather than catch the falling knife now.
insights.quote: When he finishes blowing up, the story for Bitcoin will never have been better in terms of just like the long-term value proposition... that's when Bitcoin can finally run and do its thing

insights.claim: Jonah shared a Bitcoin short from ~65k based on a range break-and-retest (range from Feb 2 to ~Mar 23/Apr 6 on the weekly), targeting a cover zone of 49-53k, and notes Bitcoin just broke 60k heading toward the 50s.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: A specific technical short with defined entry/target; the break of 60k confirms the setup playing out.
insights.quote: my target here is going to be like anywhere cover from 49 to 53

insights.claim: Jonah lays out a short basket and accumulation list in crypto: short Worldcoin (near recent highs) and Solana (could see 46, a ~50% drawdown), expects Fartcoin to 'go to zero', and plans to accumulate Zcash near ~280, Cards under 10 cents, and Hype near 40-45.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Provides explicit short targets and patient buy levels; the playbook is to wait for the crash and accumulate forgotten names at extreme lows.
insights.quote: you probably want to short something like a Worldcoin instead, which is actually oddly near the highs of recent weeks. Like that's this is for sure going down, probably to the lows.

insights.claim: Jonah suggests buying Robinhood (HOOD) while shorting crypto as an asymmetric pair, because HOOD does extremely well if crypto rallies but can still perform if crypto falls due to revenue diversification beyond crypto.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: A hedged way to stay short crypto with a long leg that has its own growth driver, reducing the cost of being wrong on direction.
insights.quote: you can buy Hood and short crypto because I think there's an asymmetry there where if crypto does well, Hood does extremely well, but Hood can still do well even if crypto does poorly

insights.claim: Both hosts warn Bitcoin will not hedge a broader market implosion and will instead fall roughly twice as fast as the Nasdaq, so it offers no protection in a sell-off.
insights.speaker: host & guest
insights.so_what: Removes the 'crypto as hedge' rationale; in a risk-off event Bitcoin amplifies rather than dampens drawdowns.
insights.quote: if the broader market implodes, it's not like Bitcoin is going to hedge you. It's going to go down twice as fast as the Nasdaq.

themes.theme: Biotech as the AI real-economy bet

insights.claim: Jonah is sizing into biotech as the prime real-economy AI beneficiary, buying more ARKG and XBI on the thesis that AI-accelerated, cheaper drug discovery will cause the entire industry to re-rate.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Expresses the AI third-leg via diversified biotech ETFs rather than single names, betting on an industry-wide multiple expansion.
insights.quote: Why I'm you know, an- an- another one is... Why I'm spending all this time on biotech and why I just bought a ton more ARKG and I bought a lot more of the XBI biotech uh ETF

insights.claim: Jonah holds biotech as ETFs in roughly a 70/30 XBI-to-ARKG ratio, using XBI as the broad industry index and ARKG as the higher-octane, higher-risk/reward sleeve, explicitly avoiding single-name stock picking due to lack of predictive edge.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Concrete position sizing for playing the biotech re-rating while diversifying away single-drug/FDA risk.
insights.quote: I own them in a 70% 30% a ratio, but we will... XBI cuz it's a much larger selection... ARK G is obviously a bet on the sort of higher octane, higher risk, higher reward

insights.claim: Jonah notes ARKG is up about 8% since the prior week's show and presents a multi-year base (4-5 years) on the chart that he reads as ready to break out, citing it as a Druckenmiller-style 'buy a little to force research' position.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: A long base plus recent momentum is the technical case for the biotech re-rating beginning now.
insights.quote: ARKG is plus 8% since this since our show last week, which is great

insights.claim: Avi cautions that biotech (unlike crypto) has real fundamentals where only PhD-level biochem expertise confers edge, so retail buying biotech 'because AI' is effectively a meme-coin-style momentum trade with no objective framework to hold through a 50% drawdown.
insights.speaker: Avi (host)
insights.so_what: A risk warning: without fundamental edge, biotech longs lack conviction support on a drawdown; trade it small and tactically, not as a core hold.
insights.quote: If you're if you're buying biotech stocks because AI, ask yourself how you'll feel if you're down 50%.

insights.claim: Both hosts endorse a process rule: before holding any trade long-term, ask whether you would add to it if it goes against you, and if the honest answer is no you lack real conviction and should not hold it.
insights.speaker: host & guest
insights.so_what: A self-test for distinguishing genuine conviction positions from momentum chases that will be sold at the lows.
insights.quote: If the price goes against you, are you adding to it? If so, do you if the answer's no, then do you really have conviction in what you're doing?

insights.claim: Both hosts treat the leftward shift in New York politics as a markets/capital-flows signal, arguing a 2028 'blue wave' controlling Senate, House and executive would threaten 'set-and-forget' equity, real estate and job positions.
insights.speaker: host & guest
insights.so_what: Reframes the political risk as a portfolio-construction risk: long-duration passive positions are vulnerable to a redistribution-driven regime change.
insights.quote: the things you can set it forget are the positions that that will be fine until there's a socialist wave... that matters for your assets, that matters for your real estate decisions

insights.claim: Jonah extends the 'sell what they sell, buy what they buy' framework to politics: if socialism creeps in, short the assets that will be redistributed and buy into the state-created monopolies erected in the name of equity.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: A contrarian positioning idea for a redistribution regime: favor protected/monopoly beneficiaries over broadly held, taxable assets.
insights.quote: if socialism starts to creep into the American economy, like sell what the what the socialists are going to redistribute and buy what you know, buy into whatever monopolies they're going to create

insights.claim: Jonah stresses pre-committing to yes/no trade decisions and price alerts, and not abandoning a months-old target just because the chart looks worst at the level you planned to buy.
insights.speaker: Jonah (guest)
insights.so_what: Process discipline to avoid the common failure of waffling at your own planned entry; some of the best trades look worst at the buy point.
insights.quote: some of the best trades I've ever taken are just where it looks the worst, but I've had that target 6 months out, and we hit the target, and then we rip it

insights.claim: Both hosts argue that in crypto the winning approach is to wait through the collapse, then accumulate forgotten assets at extreme lows, exploiting the market's bipolar swings between hatred and love for easy 2-3x returns.
insights.speaker: host & guest
insights.so_what: Patience-and-accumulate is presented as the structural edge in crypto cycles; buy when sentiment is most negative.
insights.quote: when when when you get into trouble. When the market falls apart you sit and you wait and then you accumulate. That's how you win.
